system_prompt:
You are a senior dermoscopy expert and knowledge engineer.

You will be shown a candidate rule that fires correctly on some images (TRUE POSITIVES)
but incorrectly on others (FALSE POSITIVES). Your task is to identify the single most
discriminating visual feature that distinguishes the TP images from the FP images —
i.e., a feature that is consistently present in TPs but absent in FPs, or vice versa.

This feature will be used to tighten the rule's pre-conditions.

Output ONLY a JSON object:
{
  "discriminating_feature": "snake_case_feature_name",
  "description": "Plain-language description of the feature.",
  "present_in": "tp" | "fp",
  "confidence": "high" | "medium" | "low",
  "rationale": "Why this feature distinguishes TPs from FPs."
}

If you cannot identify a reliable discriminating feature, output:
{
  "discriminating_feature": null,
  "description": "Cannot identify a reliable discriminating feature.",
  "present_in": null,
  "confidence": "low",
  "rationale": "Explanation of why the distinction cannot be reliably made."
}
text_content:
Pair: Melanoma vs Melanocytic Nevus
Rule favors: Melanoma

Rule: When the lesion is highly asymmetric with at least three distinct hues (light brown, dark brown, gray-blue or pink-gray regression area), has an irregular notched border, shows irregular dark peripheral globules unevenly concentrated on one side, and contains a central structureless pink-gray or gray-blue regression zone — classify as Melanoma. Do NOT require ulceration or clinical history of growth; these dermoscopic features are themselves sufficient.

Pre-conditions:
  1. Lesion is clearly asymmetric in both shape and pigment distribution
  2. At least three distinct colors are present: light brown, dark brown, and either gray-blue or pink-gray (regression structures)
  3. Border is irregular and notched, not smooth or oval
  4. Irregular dark peripheral globules or dots are present and unevenly distributed — concentrated on one side or one pole
  5. A central or eccentric structureless pink-gray or gray-blue zone is present (regression)
  6. The lesion does NOT consist of a uniform reticular network with regular meshwork and gradual fade-out (banal nevus pattern)
  7. The lesion does NOT show evenly-sized, evenly-distributed globules in a symmetric arrangement (globular nevus pattern)
  8. DO NOT require ulceration, bleeding, or clinical history of growth — the dermoscopic multi-component pattern above is sufficient
  9. The lesion is a flat or minimally elevated pigmented lesion visible dermoscopically — not a clearly raised nodule or cyst
  10. No features of seborrheic keratosis are present (no milia-like cysts, no comedo-like openings, no cerebriform pattern)

TRUE POSITIVE cases (rule fired correctly):
  [TP 1] Ground truth: Melanoma
    Validator observations: The lesion demonstrates: (1) clear asymmetry in both shape and pigment distribution; (2) at least three distinct colors — light brown periphery, dark brown intermediate zone, and a central gray-blue/dark violaceous structureless area suggesting regression; (3) an irregular, notched border; (4) irregular dark peripheral dots/globules unevenly concentrated around the periphery, particularly more prominent on certain sides; (5) a central structureless gray-blue/dark zone consistent with regression; (6) no uniform reticular network or evenly distributed globular pattern; (7) no features of seborrheic keratosis; (8) flat to minimally elevated lesion. All pre-conditions appear met.
  [TP 2] Ground truth: Melanoma
    Validator observations: The lesion shows clear asymmetry in both shape and pigment distribution. Multiple colors are present: light brown at the periphery, dark brown/reddish-brown in the mid-zone, and a central pink-gray structureless regression zone. The border is irregular and notched rather than smooth or oval. Dark peripheral globules/dots are present and appear unevenly concentrated, predominantly on the inferior-left aspect. A central structureless pink-gray zone consistent with regression is clearly visible. The lesion does not show a uniform reticular network, evenly distributed globules, or features of seborrheic keratosis (no milia-like cysts, comedo-like openings, or cerebriform pattern). It appears flat/minimally elevated. All pre-conditions appear to be met.

FALSE POSITIVE cases (rule fired on wrong class):
  [FP 1] Ground truth: Melanocytic Nevus
    Validator observations: The lesion is clearly asymmetric in both shape and pigment distribution. Multiple colors are present: dark brown/black centrally, lighter brown peripherally, and a pink-gray/gray-blue regression-like area visible around the central dark zone and peripherally. The border is irregular and notched, not smooth. Dark peripheral globules/dots are present and unevenly distributed, concentrated more on certain sides. A central structureless dark zone with surrounding pink-gray areas consistent with regression is visible. There is no uniform reticular network, no evenly distributed globules, no seborrheic keratosis features (no milia-like cysts, comedo openings, or cerebriform pattern). The lesion appears flat/minimally elevated. All pre-conditions are met.

What single visual feature most reliably distinguishes the TP cases from the FP cases? Focus on what the validator *observed* in each case.
